nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q1.alt: Question 1. What does the word “crouched” mean as it is used in paragraph 6? Choices: A: spun around B: bent down C: stood up D: fell over

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q2.alt: Question 2. In paragraph 9, what is the most likely reason Riley stays quiet as she and Craig walk home? Choices: A: She is thinking about how well she played hockey. B: She is upset with Craig because he hurt her back. C: She is thinking about how to get skates for Craig. D: She is tired from skating in the hockey game.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q3.alt: Question 3. What does paragraph 12 help the reader understand about Craig? Choices: A: Craig is too young to learn how to skate. B: Craig is very excited about learning to skate. C: Craig is unable to take the skates off by himself. D: Craig is worried that his sister will take the skates back.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q4.alt: Question 4. In paragraph 16, what does the phrase “like being a snail” help the reader to understand about Craig? Choices: A: He skates very slowly. B: He moves in a crooked line. C: He searches for a place to hide. D: He looks like all the other skaters.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q5.alt: Question 5. Which sentence best describes how paragraph 6 relates to paragraph 23? Choices: A: Paragraph 6 provides a problem and paragraph 23 provides a solution. B: Paragraph 6 asks a question and paragraph 23 provides an answer. C: Paragraph 6 provides a cause and paragraph 23 shows an effect. D: Paragraph 6 provides similarities and paragraph 23 shows differences.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q6.alt: Question 6. Which sentence best describes a central message of the story? Choices: A: Change is normal and an important part of life. B: Friendships often become stronger over time. C: New experiences can be exciting and wonderful. D: Natural talent is more important than practice.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q7.alt: Question 7. According to paragraph 5, what happens right before thunder can be heard? Choices: A: Little drops of water move around in the sky. B: Dark clouds appear in the sky. C: Rain droplets start to fall from the clouds. D: The air spreads because of heat from lightning.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q8.alt: Question 8. What is the main idea of paragraph 6? Choices: A: Lightning can reach from the sky to the ground. B: A bolt of lightning can travel up to nine miles. C: Flashes of lightning can jump from one cloud to another. D: Lightning can move over large distances very quickly.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q9.alt: Question 9. Which idea from the passage does the second illustration best support? Choices: A: Lightning can be helpful for nature. B: Lightning moves very quickly. C: Lightning appears in different ways in the sky. D: Lightning may strike before or after it rains.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q10.alt: Question 10. What does the word “nutrient” mean as it is used in paragraph 10? Choices: A: a type of lightning B: a supply of heat C: a kind of plant D: a form of food

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q11.alt: Question 11. Which question does the section “Fast Facts” help to answer? Choices: A: How does lightning help the earth? B: How is lightning different in the summer? C: How is electricity created in clouds? D: How can someone avoid an electrical shock?

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q12.alt: Question 12. Which sentence shows a cause and effect relationship that is stated in the passage? Choices: A: People see lightning before they hear thunder. B: Wildfires can start when lightning touches the ground. C: Heat from electricity is hotter than the surface of the sun. D: Lightning bolts can hit tall trees and buildings.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q13.alt: Question 13. What does the word “clamped” mean as it is used in paragraph 4? Choices: A: smelled B: lowered C: moved D: grabbed

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q14.alt: Question 14. Which detail from the story best shows the narrator’s point of view about catching crabs for supper? Choices: A: “My clothes were already sticking to me, and sweat trickled down our faces.” (paragraph 7) B: “This smells disgusting. How can they eat this?” (paragraph 8) C: “. . . cracking the shells and pulling out the sweet white meat. We had to keep trying.” (paragraph 11) D: “. . . but that crab was all claws and those mad pincers were waving all over the place!” (paragraph 15)

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q15.alt: Question 15. Which detail from the story best shows why Paulie and the narrator keep trying to catch a crab? Choices: A: “Crabs love stinky old fish, so our first crab didn’t take long to come.” (paragraph 4) B: “‘Crabs are scaredy-cats,’ I said, pulling up the line to check the bait.” (paragraph 8) C: “I could tell he was trying not to cry.” (paragraph 19) D: “We both thought about broccoli casserole.” (paragraph 21)

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q16.alt: Question 16. What happens right before Paulie and the narrator reach the porch in paragraph 30? Choices: A: They learn that they will have broccoli casserole for dinner. B: They hand a bucket of crabs to their father. C: They find out that something is moving in the bucket. D: They hear their mother calling them to come home.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q17.alt: Question 17. How does paragraph 31 connect to the problem in paragraph 26? Choices: A: It shows the solution to the problem. B: It gives an effect of the problem. C: It explains the cause of the problem. D: It gives an example of the problem.

nysed-ela-2021-g3-mc-q18.alt: Question 18. What best states the lesson of paragraphs 27 through 31? Choices: A: Hard work and a strong will can solve almost any problem. B: Good things can happen even when all hope seems lost. C: Staying calm is important when faced with a problem. D: Working with and trusting others can help to finish any job.
